units.title: Unit 1: Basics

lessons.title: Greetings
lessons.culturalNote: "Sawubona" literally means "I see you". It carries the spirit of ubuntu — acknowledging another person's humanity. The reply is often "Yebo, sawubona" (Yes, I see you too). Greet one person with Sawubona, and a group with Sanibonani.

exercises.prompt: You are leaving a friend's house. They say to you:

lessons.title: Yes, No & Politeness
lessons.culturalNote: Politeness opens doors in South Africa. "Ngiyabonga" (thank you) and "Ngicela" (I request / please) are used constantly. "Uxolo" means both "sorry" and "excuse me".

exercises.meaning: Excuse me, may I have water.

lessons.title: Family & Respect
lessons.culturalNote: In Zulu culture you show respect (hlonipha) by addressing elders as "Baba" (father) or "Mama" (mother) even if they are not your parents. Never call an elder by their first name only.

exercises.prompt: How do you respectfully address an older woman?

exercises.meaning: This child is five years old.

lessons.title: Numbers 1–10
lessons.culturalNote: Zulu counting words change shape depending on what you are counting (noun classes). The forms here are the neutral counting forms you'll use when counting out loud or stating a number.

lessons.title: Food & Drink
lessons.culturalNote: Sharing food is central to Zulu hospitality. If you visit a home, you may be offered "itiye" (tea) and "isinkwa" (bread). Accepting is a sign of respect.

exercises.meaning: Please may I have tea and bread.

lessons.title: Directions
lessons.culturalNote: When asking for directions, start with "Uxolo" (excuse me) to be polite. People will often walk you part of the way — a small kindness that is common across South Africa.

units.canDo: I can greet people and use basic courtesies (hello, please, thank you, yes/no).

units.title: Unit 2: Conversation

lessons.title: Introducing Yourself
lessons.culturalNote: When you meet someone, sharing your name and clan name (isibongo) builds trust. "Igama lami ngu..." means "My name is...".

exercises.prompt: Someone asks "Ngubani igama lakho?" — they want to know your:

lessons.title: Where Are You From?
lessons.culturalNote: "Uvelaphi?" (Where are you from?) is a warm conversation starter. People are proud of their home town or province.

exercises.meaning: Where are you from? I'm from Johannesburg.

lessons.title: At the Shop
lessons.culturalNote: "Yimalini?" (How much is it?) is essential at the spaza shop or market. Bargaining is polite at informal markets, but not at supermarkets.

exercises.prompt: You want to know the price. You ask:

exercises.meaning: I want water, how much is it?

lessons.title: Taxis & Asking for Help
lessons.culturalNote: Minibus taxis are South Africa's main transport. Each route has a hand sign. "Ngicela usizo" (I need help) and "Ngilahlekile" (I am lost) are phrases worth knowing by heart.

exercises.prompt: In a taxi, to get off you say:

exercises.meaning: Where is the taxi to Durban?
I need help, I am lost

units.canDo: I can introduce myself, say where I'm from, shop, and ask for help.

units.title: Unit 3: Everyday Life

lessons.title: Days of the Week
lessons.culturalNote: The Zulu week revolves around iSonto (Sunday, which also means 'week' and 'church'). Many day names are built on counting: uLwesibili (second day) through uLwesihlanu (fifth day).

lessons.title: Colours
lessons.culturalNote: Colour words in Zulu change to agree with the noun. The forms here are the common stems. 'luhlaza' covers both green and blue — say 'luhlaza okwotshani' (like grass) for green and 'luhlaza okwesibhakabhaka' (like the sky) for blue.

lessons.title: Weather & Nature
lessons.culturalNote: Weather is a favourite small-talk topic. 'Kuyashisa' (it's hot) and 'kuyabanda' (it's cold) describe the day's feeling — the 'ku-' is an impersonal 'it'.

lessons.title: Feelings
lessons.culturalNote: To say how you feel, start with 'ngi-' (I). 'Ngilambile' (I'm hungry) and 'ngomile' (I'm thirsty) are useful at any home you visit — saying them politely is normal among friends and family.

exercises.prompt: How do you say “I am tired”?

lessons.title: The Body
lessons.culturalNote: Body-part words appear in many everyday phrases. Note the noun classes: 'ikhanda' (head) and 'isandla' (hand) take different plurals — amakhanda, izandla.

units.canDo: I can talk about days, colours, weather, feelings and the body.

units.title: Unit 4: Out & About

lessons.title: Time & Routine
lessons.culturalNote: Zulu marks the day in broad parts: 'ekuseni' (morning), 'ntambama' (afternoon) and 'ebusuku' (night). 'Manje' (now) and 'kamuva' (later) help you make plans.

lessons.title: Animals
lessons.culturalNote: Animals appear in many Zulu idioms and clan praises (izithakazelo). 'Inkomo' (cattle) holds deep cultural value — it features in lobola and ceremonies.

lessons.title: Around Town
lessons.culturalNote: Knowing key places helps you get around. Start a question with 'Iphi...?' (Where is...?) — e.g. 'Iphi isikole?' (Where is the school?).

lessons.title: Health & Help
lessons.culturalNote: If you're unwell, 'Ngiyagula' (I am sick) and 'Ngicela usizo' (I need help) are vital. 'Umuthi' means medicine — it can also refer to traditional remedies.

units.canDo: I can talk about time, animals, places in town, and getting help.

units.title: Unit 5: Work & School

lessons.title: At School
lessons.culturalNote: School vocabulary is useful for kids and parents alike. 'uthisha' (teacher) and 'umfundi' (learner) are everyday words in any classroom.

lessons.title: Jobs
lessons.culturalNote: Talking about work helps you connect. Many job words borrow English roots, like 'iphoyisa' (police) — but 'umlimi' (farmer) and 'umpheki' (cook) are pure Zulu.

lessons.title: Telling Time
lessons.culturalNote: Time words help you make plans. Note 'inyanga' means both 'month' and 'moon', and 'iviki' (week) sits between 'usuku' (day) and 'unyaka' (year).

lessons.title: Useful Verbs
lessons.culturalNote: Verbs in Zulu start with 'uku-' (to ...). Learn these and you can build real sentences: 'Ngifuna ukufunda' — I want to read.

units.canDo: I can talk about school, jobs, telling the time, and use common verbs.

units.title: Unit 6: Home & Community

lessons.title: In the Home
lessons.culturalNote: 'Ikhaya' (home) is more than a house in Zulu culture — it's family and belonging. Knowing the rooms and furniture helps you feel at home anywhere.

lessons.title: Clothing
lessons.culturalNote: Clothes can carry meaning — 'ingubo' can mean a dress, clothing or a blanket given with respect. Everyday items like 'izicathulo' (shoes) are good to know.

lessons.title: Nature & Places
lessons.culturalNote: South Africa's landscapes run from 'intaba' (mountains) to 'ulwandle' (the sea). 'Idolobha' (town) and 'ipulazi' (farm) describe where people live.

lessons.title: Question Words
lessons.culturalNote: Questions unlock conversation. Pair them with what you know: 'Ubani igama lakho?' (Who/what is your name?), 'Kuphi?' (Where?).

units.canDo: I can describe my home, clothing and surroundings, and ask questions.

units.title: Unit 7: Saying More

lessons.title: Action Verbs
lessons.culturalNote: More 'uku-' verbs to build sentences. 'Ngiyakuthanda' (I love/like you) comes from 'ukuthanda'; 'ngicela ukusiza' uses 'ukusiza' (to help).

lessons.title: Fruit & Veg
lessons.culturalNote: Market words you'll use often. Many are borrowed and easy: 'ihhabhula' (apple), 'ubhanana' (banana), 'iwolintshi' (orange).

lessons.title: In the Kitchen
lessons.culturalNote: Setting the table: 'ipuleti' (plate), 'inkomishi' (cup), 'isipunu' (spoon). 'Usawoti' (salt) and 'ushukela' (sugar) season the meal.

lessons.title: Bigger Numbers
lessons.culturalNote: Past ten, Zulu builds numbers up: 'ishumi nanye' (eleven), 'amashumi amabili' (twenty), 'ikhulu' (hundred).

units.canDo: I can use action verbs, name food and kitchen items, and count higher.

units.title: Unit 8: Getting Around & People

lessons.title: Transport
lessons.culturalNote: Getting around: 'imoto' (car), 'ibhasi' (bus), 'isitimela' (train). The minibus 'itekisi' you already know is still king of the road.

lessons.title: People & Family
lessons.culturalNote: Beyond the close family: 'umngane' (friend), 'umalume' (uncle), 'umzala' (cousin). 'Umyeni' is husband; 'inkosikazi' is wife.

lessons.title: Sports & Fun
lessons.culturalNote: Play and music bring people together. 'Ibhola' (soccer) is a national passion; 'ukudlala' means to play; 'umculo' is music.

lessons.title: Helpful Phrases
lessons.culturalNote: Phrases that save the day: 'Angiqondi' (I don't understand), 'Ngiyaxolisa' (I'm sorry), 'Ukhuluma isiNgisi?' (Do you speak English?).

units.canDo: I can talk about transport, people and sport, and use helpful phrases.

lessons.title: Joining ideas
lessons.culturalNote: Small words that let you link sentences: 'Ngilambile kodwa ngiyaphila' (I'm hungry but I'm well).

reading.title: Sawubona, Thabo!
reading.intro: A short greeting between two friends. Tap any line to hear it.

lines.en: Hello Thabo. My name is Nandi.
I am well, thank you. And you?
I am also well. Go well!

reading.title: Esitolo (At the Shop)
reading.intro: Sipho buys bread and water at the spaza shop.

lines.en: Yes. Do you also want water?
Yes, I would like water. How much is it?
Here is the money. Thank you!

reading.title: Usuku Oluhle (A Good Day)
reading.intro: Nandi talks about her morning. Tap any line to hear it.

lines.en: Today is Friday. The sun is shining.
But I am hungry. I want food.
Mom is cooking meat and bread.
I am grateful. Thank you, mom!

grammar.title: Saying I, you, we
grammar.tip: Zulu verbs begin with a subject prefix: ngi- (I), u- (you), si- (we), ba- (they). From -phila (be well): ngiyaphila (I'm well), uyaphila (you're well), siyaphila (we're well).

grammar.title: One and many (plurals)
grammar.tip: Zulu nouns change their prefix to make a plural: um- people become aba-, isi- things become izi-, in- become izin-, and um- (class 3) becomes imi-.

dialogues.title: At the spaza shop
dialogues.goal: Buy what you need and ask the price

options.why: 'Hamba kahle' means 'go well' — it's a goodbye, not a greeting. Answer a greeting with 'Sawubona'.
'Cha' means 'no'. Someone greeted you — greet them back with 'Sawubona'.

turns.speaker: you
turns.prompt: Ask for what you want — bread or water

options.why: 'Ngiyabonga' says thank you — but you haven't asked for anything yet. Use 'Ngicela …' (please may I have …).
'Uvelaphi?' asks where someone comes from. To ask a price, use 'Yimalini?'
'Unjani?' asks how someone is. For a price, ask 'Yimalini?'
'Ngilambile' means 'I'm hungry' — you already have your food! Say thanks: 'Ngiyabonga'.
That asks for help — you're all done here. Thank them and greet goodbye.
'Yebo' means 'yes'. Nandi greeted you — greet her back with 'Sawubona'.
'Kude' means 'far'. Answer a greeting with a greeting: 'Sawubona!'

turns.speaker: you
turns.prompt: Say you're well and ask back

options.why: That asks 'how much?' — but Nandi asked how you ARE. Say 'Ngiyaphila' (I'm well).
'Cha' is 'no'. 'Unjani?' asks how you are — answer 'Ngiyaphila, wena?'

turns.speaker: npc
turns.name: Nandi
turns.en: I'm also well. What's your name?

options.why: That says where you're from — she asked your NAME. Use 'Igama lami ngu…'
'Ngiyabonga' is 'thank you'. She asked your name — 'Igama lami ngu…'
'Sawubona' is 'hello' — but you're leaving! Say 'Sala kahle' (stay well).
'I'm hungry' isn't a goodbye. 'Sala kahle' is how you take your leave.
